content.caption: Orbit — distribute Docker containers across a cluster of machines with no Kubernetes, no cloud.

content.text: **Orbit** is a from-scratch container orchestrator written in Go. Spin up a master node, join workers to it, and Orbit schedules Docker containers across the cluster — with multiple scheduling strategies, health monitoring, auto-removal of dead workers, and a Docker Compose-style deploy file.

content.title: Think Baby Kubernetes
content.text: Built entirely by hand in Go — stdlib only for networking and HTTP, no frameworks. Every scheduling decision, health check, and task registry update is implemented from scratch.

The **master node** owns the scheduler and task registry. Workers register with a 512-bit join key, expose their own HTTP API, and receive container run/stop commands from the master. `orbitctl` is the CLI that talks to the master over HTTP.

content.title: Project Structure
content.text: `cmd/master` – scheduler + HTTP API · `cmd/worker` – Docker runner + HTTP API · `cmd/orbitctl` – CLI · `internal/common` – shared types (Task, Node, PortBinding, OrbitCompose)

items: **Multi-node cluster** — join as many worker machines as you want.
**3 scheduling strategies** — least-loaded, round-robin, random (per task).
**Full container config** — env vars, port bindings, memory/CPU limits, restart policies, CMD override.
**orbit-compose.yaml** — deploy multi-service stacks with replicas, like Docker Compose but distributed.
**Health monitoring** — master pings workers every 5s, auto-removes dead ones after 3 failures.
**Task registry** — tracks every container's state, worker assignment, and container ID.
**Secure join key** — workers must present a 512-bit key to register.
**GC** — clean up exited containers across all workers in one command.

content.title: Prerequisites
content.text: Go 1.21+, Docker running on worker machines, Linux (`linux/amd64`).

content.code:
make
# builds orbit-master, orbit-worker, orbitctl into ./build/

sudo ./build/orbit-master init

# Output:
# Cluster initialized.
#
# Master Address : 192.168.1.10:31045
# Join Key       : a3f9bc74...
#
# Run on workers:
#   ./orbit-worker --master 192.168.1.10 --port 31045 --key a3f9bc74...

content.text: Config is saved to `/etc/orbit/master.yaml`. Running `init` again prints the existing config.

content.code:
# Run on each worker machine
./build/orbit-worker --master 192.168.1.10 --port 31045 --key a3f9bc74...
# [worker] joined cluster as worker-2484f39c (192.168.1.11)
# [worker] listening on :27834

# List all worker nodes
./build/orbitctl nodes

# List all tasks
./build/orbitctl ps

# Run a container
./build/orbitctl run nginx:alpine --port 8080:80 --name my-nginx

# Run with full options
./build/orbitctl run alpine:latest \
  --name my-task \
  --env APP_ENV=production \
  --env LOG_LEVEL=info \
  --port 3000:3000 \
  --memory 256 \
  --cpu 0.5 \
  --restart always \
  --scheduler round-robin

# Fetch logs
./build/orbitctl logs task-abc123
./build/orbitctl logs task-abc123 --tail 200

# Stop a task
./build/orbitctl stop task-abc123

# Stop ALL running tasks
./build/orbitctl stop --all

# Show workers and their containers
./build/orbitctl cluster

# Deploy from orbit-compose.yaml
./build/orbitctl deploy
./build/orbitctl deploy staging.yaml

# Garbage collect exited containers on all workers
./build/orbitctl gc

# Terminal 1 — master
sudo ./build/orbit-master init
sudo ./build/orbit-master

# Terminal 2 — worker 1
./build/orbit-worker --master 127.0.0.1 --port <PORT> --key <KEY>

# Terminal 3 — worker 2
./build/orbit-worker --master 127.0.0.1 --port <PORT> --key <KEY>

# Terminal 4 — do stuff
./build/orbitctl nodes
./build/orbitctl deploy
./build/orbitctl ps
./build/orbitctl stop --all
./build/orbitctl gc

content.caption: Orbit distributing containers across workers using the least-loaded scheduling strategy.

items: **least-loaded** – Picks the worker with the fewest running containers. Default.
**round-robin** – Rotates through healthy workers in order.

content.text: Set per-task with the `--scheduler` flag on `orbitctl run`, or per-service in `orbit-compose.yaml`.

Deploy multi-service stacks the same way you'd use Docker Compose — but distributed across the cluster. Each service supports replicas, port bindings, env vars, restart policies, CPU/memory limits, and a per-service scheduler override.

content.code:
version: "1"

services:
  web:
    image: nginx:alpine
    replicas: 3
    ports:
      - "8080:80"
    env:
      - NGINX_HOST=localhost
    restart: unless-stopped
    memory: 128
    cpu: 0.5
    scheduler: round-robin

  cache:
    image: redis:7-alpine
    replicas: 1
    ports:
      - "6379:6379"
    restart: always
    memory: 256

  worker:
    image: alpine:latest
    replicas: 5
    cmd: ["sh", "-c", "echo job done"]
    restart: no
    scheduler: random

./build/orbitctl deploy
# Deploying 3 service(s)...
#
# SERVICE   REPLICA   TASK ID          WORKER                    STATUS
# web       0         task-a1b2c3...   worker-2484f39c...        ✓ running
# web       1         task-d4e5f6...   worker-f648f2cb...        ✓ running
# web       2         task-789abc...   worker-2484f39c...        ✓ running
# cache     0         task-def012...   worker-f648f2cb...        ✓ running

content.text: The master health-checks every worker every **5 seconds**. After **3 consecutive failures (15s)**:

items: The worker is removed from the cluster.
All tasks assigned to it are marked `Failed`.
New tasks will no longer be scheduled to it.

content.text: If a worker comes back online and re-registers, it rejoins the cluster cleanly.

content.title: No Auto-Rescheduling
content.text: Tasks on a dead worker are marked `Failed` but not automatically rescheduled — intentional design to avoid duplicate container side-effects. Re-deploy manually or via `orbitctl deploy`.

content.code:
POST  /register-auth     # Worker registration with join key
POST  /run               # Submit a task (full spec)
POST  /submit            # Submit a task (image only)
POST  /stop              # Stop a task by ID
GET   /tasks             # List all tasks
GET   /tasks/:id         # Get a single task
GET   /nodes             # List all workers
GET   /cluster           # Workers + their containers
GET   /health            # Master health + worker counts
POST  /deploy            # Deploy an orbit-compose.yaml
POST  /gc                # Trigger GC on all workers

GET   /health            # Liveness check
GET   /stats             # Running container count
GET   /ps                # List containers
POST  /run               # Start a container
POST  /stop              # Stop a container
POST  /kill              # Force remove a container
GET   /logs?id=<cid>&tail=N  # Fetch container logs
POST  /gc                # Clean up exited containers

content.title: Plain HTTP
content.text: The master exposes a plain HTTP API — useful for scripting or building custom tooling on top of Orbit without going through `orbitctl`.

items: **Go** – stdlib only for networking and HTTP, no frameworks.

content.title: Zero Framework Policy
content.text: Orbit uses Go's stdlib `net/http` for all networking — no Gin, no Echo, no gRPC. Every route handler, middleware, and JSON encoder is written by hand. The goal was to understand orchestration deeply, not abstract it away.
